name: Simple Machine Scavenger Hunt
description: Explore your home, school, or neighborhood to find examples of each of the six simple machines (lever, wheel and axle, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, screw). Document each discovery with a drawing or photo and explain what human capability it extends.
learning_objectives: Identify the six simple machines in everyday objects
Explain what human capability each tool extends
Classify tools by simple machine type
variations: For younger learners: focus on just levers and inclined planes
For older learners: calculate mechanical advantage for each discovery
Team version: compete to find the most examples in a time limit

name: Build a Lever to Lift a Book
description: Using a ruler and a pencil (or similar fulcrum), build a lever and experiment with lifting a book. Move the fulcrum to different positions and observe how the force required changes. Record observations about the relationship between fulcrum position, effort, and load.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate how a lever works
Explain the relationship between fulcrum position and mechanical advantage
Predict how changing the fulcrum position will affect the force needed
variations: For younger learners: use a seesaw or balance beam for larger-scale exploration
For older learners: measure and calculate mechanical advantage ratios
Extension: design a lever system to lift a specific weight with minimal effort

name: Map a System (Breakfast to Table)
description: Choose a breakfast item (cereal, toast, orange juice) and trace its journey from origin to your table. Map every step: growing, harvesting, processing, packaging, transporting, storing, purchasing, preparing. Identify inputs, outputs, and processes at each stage. Mark at least one feedback loop and one potential unintended consequence.
materials: reference materials (optional: internet access for supply chain research)
learning_objectives: Map a complete real-world system with inputs, outputs, and processes
Identify feedback loops in supply chain systems
Recognize that simple products involve complex technological systems
variations: For younger learners: trace just three steps (farm, store, kitchen)
For older learners: add cost, energy, and environmental impact at each stage
Group version: each student traces a different item, then compare systems

name: Feedback Loop Identification Game
description: Present scenario cards describing real-world situations (thermostat heating a room, population growth, social media engagement, predator-prey cycles, bank interest). For each scenario, identify whether the feedback loop is positive (amplifying) or negative (stabilizing), draw the loop diagram, and predict what happens if the feedback is removed.
materials: colored pencils (two colors for positive/negative loops)
learning_objectives: Distinguish between positive and negative feedback loops
Diagram feedback loops with correct arrows and labels
Predict system behavior when feedback is present or absent
variations: For younger learners: use only physical/mechanical examples (thermostat, cruise control)
For older learners: analyze compound feedback systems with multiple interacting loops
Competition version: teams race to correctly classify and diagram scenarios

name: Design a Better Backpack Strap
description: Walk through the full engineering design cycle to improve a backpack strap. Research the problem (why do straps hurt shoulders?), brainstorm at least five solutions, sketch three prototypes, build one using available materials, test it with a loaded backpack, evaluate results, and document one improvement for a second iteration.
materials: a backpack with books for testing
learning_objectives: Complete a full design cycle from problem identification through iteration
Generate multiple solutions before committing to one
Test a prototype and use data to guide improvements
variations: For younger learners: redesign a simpler object (pencil grip, bookmark)
For older learners: add constraints (must cost less than $2 in materials, must be washable)
Extension: present the design to others and incorporate their feedback

name: Paper Bridge Engineering Challenge
description: Using only paper and tape, design and build a bridge that spans a 30 cm gap between two stacks of books and holds the most weight possible. Start by brainstorming designs (flat, folded, rolled, arched). Build, test by stacking coins or small weights, record the maximum load, then iterate to improve the design.
materials: coins or small weights for testing
learning_objectives: Apply the design process to a constrained engineering problem
Understand how shape and structure affect strength
Iterate on a design based on test results
variations: For younger learners: no span requirement, just build the strongest bridge
For older learners: add constraints (minimum 5 cm clearance underneath, no tape allowed)
Competition: compare designs across teams and discuss why some performed better

name: How Does the Internet Work? Trace a Message
description: Follow a text message from the moment you type it to the moment it appears on a friend's screen. Diagram each step: keyboard input, character encoding (ASCII/Unicode), packetization, local network routing, internet routing through multiple hops, destination network delivery, reassembly, and display. Label what happens at each step and what technology makes it possible.
learning_objectives: Trace information flow through a digital network from sender to receiver
Explain the concepts of encoding, packetization, and routing
Understand why messages can travel across the world in milliseconds
variations: For younger learners: simplify to 5 steps (type, send, travel, receive, display)
For older learners: include error handling, packet loss, retransmission
Physical version: students act as routers, passing folded-paper packets through the classroom

name: Binary Number Decoder
description: Learn how computers represent numbers using only 0s and 1s. Start with cards showing powers of 2 (1, 2, 4, 8, 16). Practice converting numbers between decimal and binary. Then decode a secret message by converting binary numbers to ASCII characters. Discover that everything in a computer — text, images, sounds — is represented as numbers.
learning_objectives: Convert numbers between decimal and binary representations
Understand that computers store all information as binary numbers
Decode a binary-encoded text message using an ASCII chart
variations: For younger learners: focus on binary counting (0-15) with physical cards only
For older learners: extend to hexadecimal and explore image encoding (pixels as RGB values)
Team challenge: encode and decode secret messages between teams

name: Technology Impact Timeline
description: Choose a major technology (wheel, printing press, steam engine, electricity, telephone, internet) and create an annotated timeline showing its effects on society over decades or centuries. For each time period, identify at least one positive effect, one negative effect, and one unexpected consequence. Connect your technology to at least two other technologies it influenced or was influenced by.
learning_objectives: Trace the societal impact of a technology across time
Identify both positive and negative consequences of technological change
Connect technologies to their historical predecessors and successors
variations: For younger learners: focus on one technology with three time points (invention, widespread use, today)
For older learners: compare two technologies and analyze how they amplified each other's impact
Group version: each student traces a different technology, then connect the timelines

name: Ethical Dilemma Discussion
description: Present a technology ethics scenario (self-driving car must choose between two harmful outcomes, facial recognition in public spaces, gene editing for disease prevention vs. enhancement, AI-generated content and authenticity). Using a structured framework, identify all stakeholders, list arguments for and against, consider who benefits and who is harmed, and write a position statement with supporting evidence.
learning_objectives: Identify multiple stakeholders affected by a technology decision
Construct arguments for and against a technology practice
Write a nuanced position statement that acknowledges trade-offs
variations: For younger learners: use simpler scenarios (should everyone have to wear a tracking device for safety?)
For older learners: add policy proposal writing (what rules should govern this technology?)
Debate format: teams argue opposing positions, then switch sides
